scenarios.subject: ⚠️ Urgent: Your Account Has Been Suspended
scenarios.body:
Dear Valued Customer,

We have detected suspicious activity on your Chase account and have temporarily suspended it for your protection.

To restore access, please verify your identity immediately:

Verify Account → http://chase-secure-login.net/verify

This link expires in 24 hours. Failure to verify will result in permanent account closure.

Chase Security Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Chase's real emails come from @chase.com, not @chase-verify.com. The link goes to chase-secure-login.net — not chase.com. Real banks never threaten to permanently close your account if you don't click a link in an email.
redFlags: Link goes to chase-secure-login.net, not chase.com

scenarios.subject: Your March Statement is Ready
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Your Bank of America statement for March 2026 is now available to view online.

Log in at bankofamerica.com to view your statement.

Thank you for banking with us.

Bank of America
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate email. The sender is @bankofamerica.com (the real domain). It contains no suspicious links — it simply tells you to visit bankofamerica.com yourself. There is no urgency, no threats, and no request for personal information.

scenarios.subject: IMPORTANT: Confirm Your Wire Transfer of $2,500
scenarios.body:
Dear Account Holder,

A wire transfer of $2,500.00 has been initiated from your account.

If you did NOT authorize this, click below to cancel immediately:

CANCEL TRANSFER → http://wellsfargo-cancel.com/stop

You have 30 minutes before the transfer is completed and cannot be reversed.

Wells Fargo Fraud Department
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. The sender is wellsfarg0.com — the letter 'o' has been replaced with the number '0', which is easy to miss. The 30-minute countdown is designed to cause panic so you click without thinking. The link goes to wellsfargo-cancel.com, not wellsfargo.com.
redFlags: Misspelled domain: wellsfarg0.com (zero, not letter o)
Real wire disputes are handled by calling your bank directly

scenarios.subject: Your Citi Card Has Been Locked
scenarios.body:
NOTICE: Your Citi credit card ending in 4821 has been locked.

Reason: Multiple failed login attempts.

Unlock your card here:
https://citibank-update.net/unlock

You will need to provide your card number, expiration date, and CVV to unlock.

Citi Customer Service
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Citibank's real domain is citi.com or citibank.com — not citibank-update.net. More importantly, no legitimate bank will ever ask you to provide your CVV by email. The CVV exists specifically so it is never stored or requested this way.
redFlags: Requests CVV — banks never ask for this by email
Uses fear of a locked card to pressure you

scenarios.subject: Receipt for your payment to Netflix — $15.99
scenarios.body:
Hi,

You sent a payment of $15.99 USD to Netflix.

Transaction ID: 7GH29-38KD92
Date: June 14, 2026

If you didn't authorize this payment, open a dispute at paypal.com/disputes.

Thanks,
PayPal
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate PayPal receipt. The sender is @paypal.com, the amount is small and plausible, and it directs you to paypal.com/disputes rather than a suspicious link. PayPal regularly sends receipts like this for completed payments.

scenarios.subject: Capital One: Unusual Activity Detected on Your Card
scenarios.body:
Dear Customer,

We detected an unusual $899.00 purchase on your Capital One Quicksilver card at an electronics store in Florida.

If this wasn't you, click here to dispute:

DISPUTE → http://capitalone-alerts.com/dispute-now

Your card will be charged in 1 hour if no action is taken.

Capital One Fraud Prevention
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Capital One's real domain is capitalone.com, not capitalone-alerts.com. Real fraud alerts don't give you a 1-hour deadline before charging your card — they hold the charge while they investigate. Always call the number on the back of your card to verify.

scenarios.subject: Sarah paid you $25.00
scenarios.body:
Sarah Mitchell paid you $25.00

For: Dinner last night 🍕

View in app → venmo.com/account

Transfer to bank: venmo.com/transfer

Venmo
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Venmo payment notification. The sender is @venmo.com (the real domain), the amount and note are realistic for a casual payment, and the links go to venmo.com. Venmo regularly sends notifications like this when you receive money.

scenarios.subject: Your Apple Pay Refund of $349.99 is Ready
scenarios.body:
Hello,

A refund of $349.99 has been approved for your recent Apple Pay transaction.

To receive your refund, please confirm your card details:

Claim Refund → http://apple-pay-service.com/refund

Processing time: 1-2 business days after confirmation.

Apple Pay Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Apple's domain is apple.com — not apple-pay-service.com. Apple Pay refunds happen automatically through the original payment method without needing to re-enter card details. Scammers use 'refund' offers to trick you into giving up card information.
redFlags: Asks you to re-enter card details
Refunds never require confirming card info

scenarios.subject: You qualify for a $750 government relief deposit
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Based on your Cash App activity, you have been pre-approved for a $750 economic relief deposit.

To claim, link your Cash App and verify identity:

Claim Funds → http://cash-app-rewards.net/claim

All deposits must be claimed within 24 hours.

Cash App Rewards
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Cash App's real domain is cash.app, not cash-app-rewards.net. There is no government relief program that operates through Cash App pre-approvals. These scams trick you into linking your account so attackers can steal funds.

scenarios.subject: Chase Alert: Payment received for credit card ending in 8821
scenarios.body:
Hi,

We received your credit card payment of $245.00 on June 13, 2026.

New balance: $1,832.41
Next payment due: July 8, 2026

View details at chase.com or in the Chase Mobile app.

Chase
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Chase payment confirmation. The sender is @chase.com (the real domain), contains no suspicious links, and gives factual transaction information. Chase routinely sends payment confirmations like this.

scenarios.subject: HSBC: Your tax refund of $1,847 is ready for deposit
scenarios.body:
Dear HSBC Customer,

The IRS has processed your tax refund of $1,847.00 and selected HSBC for deposit.

Confirm your account number and routing number to receive your refund:

Verify Deposit → http://hsbc-tax.com/refund

Deposits must be confirmed within 48 hours.

HSBC Tax Services
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. HSBC's real domain is hsbc.com, not hsbc-tax.com. The IRS does not work with individual banks to process refunds, and refunds are deposited automatically to the account you provided on your tax return — no confirmation needed.
redFlags: Asks you to confirm account and routing numbers

scenarios.subject: You received $500 via Zelle — Verify to deposit
scenarios.body:
Good news!

Michael Johnson sent you $500 via Zelle.

To accept this payment, verify your bank account:

Accept Payment → http://zelle-verify.com/accept

Unclaimed payments are returned after 14 days.

Zelle
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. Zelle's real domain is zellepay.com. Real Zelle transfers deposit automatically into your account if you're already enrolled — there is no separate 'accept payment' link to click. Scammers use this 'free money' approach to get banking credentials.

scenarios.subject: Your Discover statement is now available
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Your Discover card statement closing June 12, 2026 is now available.

Balance: $432.17
Minimum payment: $35.00
Due date: July 9, 2026

Log in to discover.com to view your full statement.

Discover
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Discover statement notification. The sender is @discover.com (the real domain), the message contains no clickable suspicious links, and it directs you to log in to discover.com directly.

scenarios.subject: Your U.S. Bank debit card has been compromised
scenarios.body:
URGENT,

Your U.S. Bank debit card has been involved in a data breach. To prevent unauthorized charges, we must verify your card details immediately.

Verify Card → http://usbank-secure.com/verify

We will need:
• Full card number
• Expiration date
• PIN

Failure to verify within 12 hours will result in permanent card cancellation.

U.S. Bank Fraud Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. U.S. Bank's real domain is usbank.com, not usbank-secure.com. No legitimate bank will ever ask for your PIN by email — your PIN is something only you should ever know. Banks block compromised cards automatically and send you a new one.
redFlags: Asks for your PIN — never share this
Asks for full card number via email

scenarios.subject: Last chance: Lock in 12% guaranteed returns on Bitcoin
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Our exclusive crypto investment program is closing tonight at midnight.

Deposit minimum $500 USD to receive guaranteed 12% monthly returns.

Deposit now → http://crypto-investments-pro.com/deposit

Testimonial: 'I made $50,000 in 3 months!' — John D.

Secure your future today.

Crypto Investments Pro
scenarios.explanation: This is a classic investment scam. No legitimate investment offers 'guaranteed' returns — all investments carry risk. Promises of unrealistic returns combined with deadline pressure and fake testimonials are hallmarks of Ponzi-style fraud.
redFlags: Promises guaranteed returns (no investment is guaranteed)

scenarios.subject: Your payment is due on June 30
scenarios.body:
Hello,

This is a reminder that your American Express card payment is due on June 30, 2026.

Current balance: $1,204.55
Minimum due: $35.00

Schedule a payment by logging in at americanexpress.com or in the Amex app.

Thank you for being a valued Card Member.

American Express
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Amex payment reminder. The sender is @americanexpress.com (the real domain), there are no suspicious links, and it provides factual account information without asking you to click anything urgent.

scenarios.subject: PNC: Your online banking has been disabled
scenarios.body:
Dear PNC Customer,

Due to suspicious activity, your PNC online banking access has been disabled.

To re-enable access, please log in and verify your identity:

Re-enable Access → http://pnc-online-banking.com/login

Your account will be permanently locked in 24 hours if no action is taken.

PNC Security
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. PNC's real domain is pnc.com, not pnc-online-banking.com. The 'login' link leads to a fake page designed to steal your credentials. If your access were truly disabled, you would call the bank — not click a link in an email.

scenarios.subject: Your PayPal account has been limited
scenarios.body:
Dear PayPal user,

We have placed a limitation on your account due to unusual activity.

To remove this limitation, log in and complete the resolution centre steps:

Resolve Now → http://paypal-resolution.com/resolve

Limitations not resolved within 72 hours may result in account closure and held funds.

PayPal Resolution Team
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. PayPal's real domain is paypal.com, not paypal-resolution.com. PayPal does have a Resolution Center, but you access it by logging in to paypal.com directly. Scammers often impersonate this exact process to steal credentials.
redFlags: Login link goes to non-paypal.com domain

scenarios.subject: We detected a sign-in from a new device
scenarios.body:
Hi,

We noticed a new login to your Robinhood account.

When: June 14, 2026, 10:32 AM
Device: Pixel 8 Pro
Location: Seattle, WA

If this was you, you can ignore this email. If not, secure your account at robinhood.com/security.

Robinhood
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Robinhood security notification. The sender is @robinhood.com (the real domain), it provides factual device and location info, and directs you to log in via robinhood.com directly — no shady links.

scenarios.subject: Pre-approved: Refinance and save $487/month — Act today!
scenarios.body:
Congratulations homeowner,

You are pre-approved to refinance your mortgage at a 2.1% rate. Save up to $487 per month.

This offer expires tonight at midnight.

Get your new rate → http://quickrefi-mortgage.com/start

No credit check required to apply.

QuickRefi Team
scenarios.explanation: This is a scam. Legitimate mortgage refinances always require a credit check and rates near 2.1% don't exist in 2026's market. Pressure tactics, unrealistic savings, and 'no credit check' are red flags. These scams collect personal info or upfront 'application fees'.
redFlags: 'No credit check' is impossible for real refis

scenarios.subject: IRS Final Notice: Levy on your bank account
scenarios.body:
FINAL NOTICE,

The IRS has issued a levy against your bank account for unpaid taxes of $4,217.66.

Your funds will be frozen in 48 hours unless payment is made.

Pay Now → http://treasury-tax-gov.com/pay

Accepted: Bitcoin, gift cards, wire transfer.

IRS Collections Department
scenarios.explanation: This is an IRS impersonation scam. The IRS never sends collection notices by email and never accepts payment in Bitcoin or gift cards. The IRS always sends paper notices through the U.S. mail first. The domain treasury-tax-gov.com is fake — the real IRS uses irs.gov.
redFlags: IRS does not contact you by email
Accepts gift cards or Bitcoin (never legitimate)

scenarios.subject: Your weekly account summary
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Here is your weekly Bank of America summary for the week ending June 13, 2026.

Checking balance: $3,421.89
Savings balance: $12,090.04
Credit card balance: $654.21

Log in at bankofamerica.com for full details.

Bank of America
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Bank of America weekly summary. The sender is @bankofamerica.com (the real domain), provides only factual account balances, and contains no clickable suspicious links or requests for personal information.

scenarios.subject: We can recover your stolen Bitcoin — Free consultation
scenarios.body:
Hello,

Our firm specializes in recovering stolen cryptocurrency. We saw your post about losing Bitcoin and want to help.

95% success rate. We only charge a small upfront fee of $500 for blockchain analysis.

Start Recovery → http://bitcoin-recovery-team.org/start

Recover your funds today.

Bitcoin Recovery Team
scenarios.explanation: This is a 'recovery scam' that targets people who have already been scammed. Once cryptocurrency is sent, it cannot be reversed or recovered. Anyone claiming to recover it for an upfront fee is taking advantage of victims a second time.
redFlags: Crypto cannot be recovered after sending
Unsolicited contact about a private loss

scenarios.subject: USAA: Suspicious login attempt blocked
scenarios.body:
Dear USAA Member,

We blocked a sign-in attempt to your USAA account from an unknown device in Russia.

If this was not you, secure your account immediately:

Secure Account → http://usaa-secure-login.com/secure

You must verify within 1 hour or your account may be compromised.

USAA Security
scenarios.explanation: This is phishing. USAA's real domain is usaa.com, not usaa-secure-login.com. Real USAA security alerts will tell you to log in to usaa.com directly or open the USAA app — not click a link to a different domain.

scenarios.subject: Your transfer of $200.00 has been completed
scenarios.body:
Hi,

Your transfer of $200.00 from Bank of America ****1234 to your Robinhood account is complete.

Funds are now available to trade.

View in app or at robinhood.com.

Robinhood
scenarios.explanation: This is a legitimate Robinhood transfer confirmation. The sender is @robinhood.com (the real domain), it confirms a routine transfer with masked account details, and directs you to log in via robinhood.com without any urgent action required.
